text: The surgeon begins dissection in the supravesicular space, extending the flap across to the contralateral side for better manipulation and visualization. The flap is carried down on top of the plug, with visible adhesion medially near the vessels. Fat is pushed laterally to protect the nerves, revealing the inferior epigastric vessels on the right. Suture material becomes visible as the recurrent hernia is delivered, with impressive contents. Electrocautery is used with gentle manipulation to separate tissues.

The surgeon operates with a camera angled at thirty degrees, alternating between up and down for optimal visualization. The surgical field includes the inguinal ligament, Cooper's ligament, and symphysis pubis. The surgeon dissects towards the mesh, splitting the flap and mesh to access the inferior epigastric vessels, while carefully avoiding nerve damage. The spermatic cord is visible, and the surgeon checks beneath the flap during the medial dissection, using it for traction against the mesh. The inferior epigastric vessels are positioned at the top, with the surgeon working laterally to ensure safety.

The surgeon cuts the flap to reduce torque on the vessels, improving visualization. The mesh is manipulated and shaved off near the spermatic cord, connecting to the medial dissection. The obturator plug is carefully pulled out, avoiding the nerve on the medial side. A pocket is created, and the mesh is sutured in place.

The surgeon places a high medial suture for direct hernia repair, using a triangular fixation technique with sutures positioned lateral to the chronomotor branch, medial to the iliac vessels, and at high medial and high lateral points.
